summary:
The primary driver of vertical maxillary growth involves resorptive-depositional changes primarily taking place along facial sutures during development; these transitions are crucial as they enable proper alignment adjustments necessary throughout ontogeny.
The emphasis here should be placed not only on bone remodeling but also integrating understanding about how specific developmental stages influence overall skeletal organization via intricate interactions between different anatomical structures including those involved directly within craniomaxillofacial regions. To further clarify this point regarding critical juncture where horizontal-to-vertical orientation shifts occur which allows sufficient space formation needed laterally expanding jaws vertically upwards towards adult configurations while maintaining structural integrity across all dimensions simultaneously achieved through coordinated actions among multiple systems working together seamlessly over time period spanning fetal life until late adolescence/early adulthood depending upon individual variations observed clinically amongst patients presenting diverse phenotypes related broadly speaking under umbrella term 'craniofacial dysmorphology'. Agent2's summary focuses mainly on explaining why vertical expansion happens based largely off evidence pointing toward activity happening specifically near joints called "sutures" instead other parts
